text:
Dear Margaret,

I've been meaning to write this for some time, and I'm not sure why I kept putting it off — perhaps because the right words felt too small for what I actually want to say.

When I first came to you, I was capable but uncertain. I knew what I could do, but I had very little sense of what I was for. You had a way of listening that made me feel not just heard but seen, and that is rarer than most people realize. You never handed me answers, but you asked exactly the questions that helped me find my own.

I think of the afternoon you told me to stop apologizing for taking up space in the room. I don't know if you remember saying it, but I do. I've carried it with me into every meeting, every negotiation, every moment when I felt myself beginning to shrink. You said it so simply, as if it were obvious, and slowly it became obvious to me too.

Your patience was a kind of generosity I didn't fully appreciate at the time. Growth is slow and often ungainly, and you never once made me feel embarrassed by mine. You held a vision of who I could become even when I couldn't hold it myself, and you were steady in that vision even when I was not.

What I want you to know is that the work I'm doing now — the work I'm proud of — carries your fingerprints on it. Not because you did it, but because you helped me believe I could.

Thank you, Margaret. For the time, the candor, the faith. I hope to be that for someone else someday, and if I manage it even halfway as well as you did, I'll consider it a life well spent.

With genuine gratitude,
David
